mechanism: Pacific frontal system driving sustained high winds over Olympic Mountain ridges; aeolian tones, background roar, tree fall, topographic amplification at saddles
status: Second entry in Storm Arc (257-260). E=8 continues. Storm moves inland from coast (257) to ridge (258).

olympic_mountains.geology: Basalt (subducted oceanic crust) and sedimentary rock; not volcanic unlike Cascades
olympic_mountains.rain_shadow_gradient: 170 inches (west slope) to 16 inches (northeast, Sequim) across approximately 60 miles

acoustic_physics.aeolian_tones: Vortex shedding from obstacles (trunks, branches, twigs, rocks). Frequency determined by diameter and wind speed (Strouhal number). Large trunks = low drones; branches = mid whistles; twigs = high hisses. Many simultaneously = multi-tonal chaos.
acoustic_physics.background_roar: Sum of thousands of turbulent events at all scales: eddies, vortices, boundary layer turbulence from every surface.
acoustic_physics.ridge_saddle_howl: Topographic venturi effect: wind accelerated through narrow gap; sound amplified by constriction. Loudest point on ridge.
acoustic_physics.tree_fall_sequence: Crack at base (wood failure) → whomp of crown hitting other trees → crash of trunk hitting ground → secondary branch sounds.
acoustic_physics.e8_criteria: Full Pacific frontal violence at ridge elevation: gusts 80-100+ mph; continuous multi-tonal roar; frequent tree fall; topographic amplification.

frontal_dynamics.pacific_frontal_types: Warm front, cold front, occluded front, atmospheric river (Pineapple Express)
frontal_dynamics.wind_maximum: At cold front boundary: steepest pressure gradients, maximum wind speeds at ridge level
frontal_dynamics.post_frontal_winds: Strong northwesterly 30-50 mph sustained, gusts to 70+ for 12-24 hours post-frontal
frontal_dynamics.sitka_spruce_vulnerability: Shallow root system due to waterlogged soil and competition. Blow over rather than break in major events. Blowdowns create chaotic acoustic events.

columbus_day_storm.max_sustained_winds_mph: 100+ at exposed Olympic Peninsula locations; possible gusts exceeding 150
columbus_day_storm.significance: Historical benchmark for maximum Pacific windstorm on Olympic Peninsula. Reference event for E=9 level occurrence.

theory_nodes.concept: Storm moves inland: same frontal system documented as Juan de Fuca Surf (coast, 257) and Olympic Peninsula Wind Storm (ridge, 258). The acoustic character transforms as the environment changes: sustained coastal roar → howling mountain ridge chaos. The energy level maintains E=8; the acoustic form changes completely.
theory_nodes.description: Storm system documentation from coast (257) to ridge (258): same energy, transformed acoustic character.

theory_nodes.concept: Aeolian tone production at ridge: multi-tonal chaos. Vortex shedding from obstacles of all sizes produces aeolian tones at all frequencies simultaneously. Olympic ridge with mixed-size trees = natural pipe organ driven by storm wind. Background roar + foreground tones + episodic tree falls = the full E=8 ridge wind soundscape.
theory_nodes.description: Aeolian multi-tonal chaos: mountain ridge as natural pipe organ in storm.

theory_nodes.concept: Columbus Day Storm (October 12, 1962) as historical benchmark. Sustained winds exceeding 100 mph; possible gusts exceeding 150 mph; 15 billion board feet blown down; 46 deaths; $235M damage (1962). The reference event for Olympic Peninsula maximum wind. Characteristic major Pacific windstorm (10-20 year recurrence) = E=8; Columbus Day Storm level = arguable E=9.
theory_nodes.description: Columbus Day Storm as the reference maximum for Olympic Peninsula wind events.

cross_references.relationship: Same frontal system, different position. Coast surf (257) → ridge wind storm (258). The storm moves inland.
Gentle aeolian wind through forest documented at E=4. Olympic Peninsula Wind Storm (E=8) is the same acoustic mechanism at maximum violence: aeolian tones in Sitka spruce at 4x the energy scale.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=8 vs E=5. Olympic ridge howling chaos vs. gospel rock fuzz warmth. The ridge cannot hear 'Spirit in the Sky'. Human optimism is smaller than the storm.

storm_arc_position.note: Storm moves inland from coast. E=8 continues. Degree 259 brings Cascade thunderstorm; degree 260 brings E=9.
